results.possibleIdentity: Harley Jones was a British filmmaker, film editor, and educator who was a prominent figure in the experimental film scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. He began his career as a film editor for Scottish Television from 1963 to 1966, working on John Grierson's program 'This Wonderful World.' In 1966, he became a co-founder and the first course leader of the Newport Film Unit (later the Newport Film School) at the Newport College of Art and Design, where he shaped the curriculum until 1982. Jones is known for his experimental and documentary films, including 'Resurrection' (1966), an impressionistic homage to steam engines, and 'After Many a Summer' (1968), which documented the redevelopment of the Tiger Bay community in Cardiff and was purchased by the Contemporary Art Society for Wales. His work frequently combined documentary styles with animation. He later worked as an independent producer for the Welsh-language channel S4C and remained active in community cinema projects into the 21st century. His films are preserved in the National Screen and Sound Archive of Wales and the BFI National Archive. Given his role at the Newport College of Art—a notable hub for experimental media and collaboration between art and technology—he is a strong candidate for an association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement or similar initiatives that flourished in art schools during the late 1960s.

results.possibleIdentity: Paul Nance is an interdisciplinary artist and engineer who has been active since the late 1960s. He describes himself as an engineer, artist, maker, writer, and teacher with a career in photography spanning over fifty years. Based in Edison, New Jersey—a historical hub for technological research like Bell Labs—he has worked as a commercial photographer for decades and has also served as a programmer and network technician. His artistic practice often bridges the gap between technology and art, a hallmark of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. He has written extensively on the technical and aesthetic evolution of photography from the 1960s to the digital age, including early experiments with homemade pinhole cameras. His background as a 'maker' and engineer, combined with his long-term practice in visual arts (specifically photography and cinematography), aligns with the collaborative spirit of the E.A.T. movement that promoted partnerships between artists and engineers.

results.possibleIdentity: Douglas White was a multimedia artist, video pioneer, and architectural historian active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was a co-founding member of the influential media collective Environmental Communications (EC), established in 1969 in Venice, California. The collective focused on documenting the urban environment, pop architecture, and experimental structures using then-emergent technologies such as time-lapse video, 16mm film, and slide photography. White was also associated with the New York-based video art scene, operating under the name Alternative Environmental Futures (at 316 West 88th St., NYC). In 1970, he was contracted to produce a video documentary of an experimental cardboard geometric environment constructed for a school in Englewood, New Jersey. His work appeared in or was cited by Radical Software, the primary journal for video art and cybernetics, which was closely linked to the ecosystem of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He collaborated with architects, designers, and media theorists, including contributions to projects involving Marshall McLuhan and documenting early environmental and technological 'Happenings'.

results.possibleIdentity: Stanley Edelman was a New York-based artist and painter who was also a prominent surgeon (Dr. Stanley Edelman). He was active in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and 1970s and continued his practice for several decades. His work as a painter has been described as "indispensable" by cultural organizations such as the Roundabout Theatre Company and the 92nd Street Y (92NY), where he has been a supporter of the arts. He is known for his involvement in the intersection of medicine and the humanities, and his artistic career ran concurrently with his medical profession. While he is primarily recognized as a painter, his dual identity as a surgeon and artist aligns with the spirit of interdisciplinary collaboration championed by organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

Seymour Edelman (also cited as S. Edelman) was a physicist and engineer at the National Bureau of Standards (NBS, now NIST) who was a pioneer in the field of piezoelectric polymers, acoustics, and vibration sensors during the late 1960s and 1970s. Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) frequently collaborated with engineers from research institutions like NBS and Bell Labs to develop advanced technologies for artistic projects. Edelman's research into piezoelectric sensors and acoustics was highly relevant to major E.A.T. initiatives, such as the Pepsi Pavilion at the 1970 World Expo in Osaka, which featured complex sensor-driven sound systems and environments. In the collaborative environment of E.A.T., engineers like Edelman were often credited alongside artists as equal contributors to the creation of technological art works.
